title: Combination of Capacitors
summary: Here the capacitance, $ C = \frac{A\pi r_{0}}{d} $ The above three capacitors $ C_{1}, C_{2}, C_{3} $ are in parallel and then it is in series with $ C_{4} $.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - This is a balanced Wheatstone bridge. The equivalent circuit is
On Solving the circuit, As $ C_{1} $ and $ C_{2} $ are in series so, $$ \frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}=\frac{1}{C} $$ $$ C^{\prime}=1~\upmu F $$ As $ C_{3} $ and $ C_{4} $ are in series $$ \frac{1}{C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}}=\frac{1}{C} $$ SO. $$ C^{n}=1~\upmu F $$ Now C'and C" are in parallel so, $ \text{C} = \text{C}' + \text{C}'' = (1 + 1)\mu\text{F} = 2\mu\text{F} $ -
In parallel combination, $$ C_{p}=3+3=6\mu\mathrm{F} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}C_{eq}&=\frac{C_{P}\times3}{3+C_{P}}=\frac{3\times6}{6+3}\\&=2\mu F\end{aligned} $$ -
Since, the capacitor (3) is shorted out by the parallel wire, therefore the net capacitance is in parallel. $$ C_{P}=C+C=2C $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\bullet\\ \bullet\end{array} $$ - Here the capacitance, $ C = \frac{A\pi r_{0}}{d} $ The above three capacitors $ C_{1}, C_{2}, C_{3} $ are in parallel and then it is in series with $ C_{4} $. Here, $$ C_{1}=\frac{\left(\frac{A}{3}\right)k_{1}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{1}}{3k}C $$ $$ C_{2}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathbf{A}}{3}\right)k_{2}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{2}}{3k}C $$ $$ C_{3}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathbf{A}}{3}\right)k_{3}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{3}}{3k}C $$ $$ C_{4}=\frac{A k_{4}\varepsilon_{0}}{\frac{d}{2}}=\frac{2k_{4}}{k}C $$ Now, the equivalent capacitance for the combination of four capacitors is, $$ \frac{1}{C_{eq}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}+C_{2}+C_{3}}+\frac{1}{C_{4}} $$ or $$ \frac{1}{C}=\frac{3k}{2C}\left[\frac{1}{k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3}}\right]+\frac{k}{2k_{4}C} $$ or $$ \mathrm{(as~C_{eq}=C)} $$ $$ \frac{2}{k}=\frac{3}{k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3}}+\frac{1}{k_{4}} $$
##### Caution Students may think that, the first three dielectrics appear to be connected in series so capacitor are also in series, but here since capacitor plates are connected together, so capacitance are connected in parallel. -
Here, $$ \mathbf{V}=3\mathbf{V} $$ $$ \frac{1}{C_{eq}}=\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}+\frac{1}{C_{3}} $$ $$ C_{eq}=\frac{C}{3} $$ - Here, $ C_{1} $, $ C_{2} $ and $ C_{3} $ are in series.
$$ \frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{2C}+\frac{1}{3C} $$ $$ \frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{6+3+2}{6C}=\frac{11}{6C} $$ $$ C^{\prime}=\frac{6C}{11} $$ All the capacitors in branch number 1 is in series, so, the charge on each number capacitor is: $$ Q^{\prime}=\frac{6}{11}CV $$ Also charge on capacitor $ C_{4} $ is, Q = 4 CV $$ \begin{aligned}Ratio}&=\frac{Q^{\prime}}{Q}=\frac{6CV}{11\times4CV}\\&=\frac{3}{22}\end{aligned} $$ - The given situation can be shown as follows.
$$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{AB}}&=(2+4)\mu\mathrm{F}\\&=6\mu\mathrm{F}\end{aligned} $$ - $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{I)V}=\frac{C_{1}V_{1}+C_{2}V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\\\Rightarrow\quad V^{\prime}=\frac{C_{1}V_{1}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\quad(\because V_{2}=0)\\ \Rightarrow\quad V^{\prime}=\frac{C_{1}V}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\quad\{\because V_{1}=V\}\end{array} $$

title: Effect of Dielectric on Capacitance
summary: Electric field in volume, $ E_{0} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_{0}} $ When plates are dipped in kerosene oil electric field, <div style="text-align: center;"><img…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - The capacitance (C) of a capacitor depends on the geometry and the dielectric medium between the plates. It is defined by the relation. $$ C=\frac{A\varepsilon_{o}}{d} $$ - Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor when medium is air, $$ C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}A}{d} $$ From $2^{\mathrm{nd}}$ condition, $$ \mathbf{A}^{\prime}=\mathbf{A},\,t=\frac{d}{2}\,,\,\mathbf{K}=4 $$ $$ \mathbf{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{(d-t)+\frac{t}{\mathbf{K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{\left(d-\frac{d}{2}\right)+\frac{d/2}{4}} $$ $$ =\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{\frac{d}{2}+\frac{d}{8}}=\frac{8}{5}.\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d} $$ $$ \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{C}_{0}}=\frac{\frac{8\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{5\mathrm{~d}}}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{d}}} $$ $$ \frac{C}{C_{0}}=\frac{8}{5} $$ - Given, $$ \mathbf{C}_{0}=6\mu\mathbf{F} $$ $$ \mathbf{C_{M}}=30~\mu\mathbf{F} $$ $\therefore$ Dielectric, $K=\frac{C_{M}}{C_{0}}=\frac{30}{6}=5$ Permittivity of medium, $$ \begin{aligned}\varepsilon_{m}&=\mathbf{K}\times\varepsilon_{\mathbf{o}}=5\times\varepsilon_{\mathbf{o}}\\&=5\times8.854\times10^{-12}\\&=0.44\times10^{-10}\mathbf{C}^{2}\mathbf{N}^{-1}\mathbf{\Phi m}^{-2}.\end{aligned} $$ - Electric field in volume, $ E_{0} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_{0}} $ When plates are dipped in kerosene oil electric field,
$$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{K}} $$ Here, K > 1 $ \therefore $ $ E < E_{0} $ So, electric field between the plates will decrease. - We have, the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with dielectric (oil) between its plates is $$ C=\frac{\mathbf{K}\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{d} $$ where, $ \varepsilon_{0} = $ electric permittivity of free space K = dielectric constant of oil A = area of each plate of capacitor d = distance between two plates When the dielectric (oil) is removed, then the capacitor's capacitance will be, $$ C_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}}{d} $$ On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $$ C=K C_{0} $$ $$ \implies $$ $$ C_{0}=\frac{C}{K}=\frac{C}{2} $$

title: Electrostatic Potential
summary: According to question, a bullet of mass 2 g is having a charge of $ 2\ \mu\text{C} $.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Given Volume, $ V = 0.2 \, \text{m}^3 $ Electric potential = 5 V (constant) Electric field = ? Electric field, $ E = -\frac{dV}{dr} $ Here $ V = 5 \, \text{V} $ and constant $ \therefore $ $ E = -\frac{d(5)}{dr} $ $ = \frac{d(\text{constant})}{dr} = 0 $
- According to question, a bullet of mass 2 g is having a charge of $ 2\ \mu\text{C} $. Here we apply, $$ \frac{1}{2}m\mathbf{v}^{2}=q\mathbf{V} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}&=\frac{1}{2}\times\frac{2\times10^{-3}\times10\times10}{2\times10^{-6}}\\&=50\mathrm{kV}\end{aligned} $$

title: Electrostatics of Conductors
summary: We know that inside the conductor, E = 0.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- We know that inside the conductor, E = 0. So, potential remains same.

title: Energy Stored in a Capacitor
summary: First, when switch S is connected to point 1.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Given C = 12 pF = 2 $ \times $ 10 $ ^{-12} $ F $$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{pF}=2\times10^{-12}\mathrm{F}\\&\mathrm{V}=50\mathrm{~V}\\&\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2}\times12\times10^{-12}\times(50)^{2}\\&\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2}\times12\times10^{-12}\times2500\\&\mathrm{U}=1.5\times10^{-8}\mathrm{J}\\&\mathrm{U}=15\mathrm{nJ}\\ \end{aligned} $$ - Given: $$ \begin{array}{l}C_{1}=C_{2}=900\ pF\\ V_{1}=100\ V,V_{2}=0\ V\end{array} $$ When $ C_{1} $ and $ C_{2} $ are joined, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{\mathbf{C}_{1}\mathbf{V}_{1}+\mathbf{C}_{2}\mathbf{V}_{2}}{\mathbf{C}_{1}+\mathbf{C}_{2}}\\&=\frac{900\times100+0}{1800}=50\mathrm{~V}\end{aligned} $$ Energy, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{U}&=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2})\mathrm{V}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times1800\times10^{-12}\times50\times50\\&=2.25\times10^{-6}\mathrm{J}\end{aligned} $$ - Given, Electric Field = E Distance between plates = d Area of plates = A Energy = ? We know, $\frac{\mathrm{Energy}}{\mathrm{Volume}} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}$ $$ \mathrm{Energy}=\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}\times\mathrm{Volume} $$ $$ \mathrm{E n e r g y}=\mathrm{~\frac{1}{2}~}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}\mathrm{A}d $$ - We know from question,
$$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2} $$ On switching key at point C, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{V}_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{2\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{V}^{2}\\=\frac{1}{4}\mathrm{CV}^{2}\end{array} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text{Loss} =\frac{\mathbf{V}_{i}-\mathbf{V}_{f}}{\mathbf{V}_{i}}\times100 \\ =\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\right)\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}}\times100=50\% \end{array} $$ - The given scenario can be represented as follows
Charge on capacitor. $$ \boldsymbol{q}=\boldsymbol{C}\boldsymbol{V} $$ When it is connected with another uncharged Capacitor, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{q+0}{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2} $$ Initial energy, $$ \mathbf{U}_{i}=\frac{1}{2}c\mathbf{v}^{2} $$ ∴ Final energy. $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{U}_{f}=2\times\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{C}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2}\right)^{2}\\=\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2}}{4}\end{array} $$ $\therefore$ Loss of energy, $U_{i}-U_{f}=\frac{CV^{2}}{4}$ i.e., decreases by a factor 2. - First, when switch S is connected to point 1. Then,
Initial energy stored in capacitor 2 $ \mu $F is, $$ \mathbf{U}_{i}=\frac{1}{2}(2\mu\mathbf{F})\mathbf{V}^{2}\left[\mathbf{E}=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}\right] $$ Second, when switch S is connected to point 2, then $$ \mathrm{V}_{f}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{2\mathrm{V}}{10}=0.2\mathrm{~V} $$ ∴ Final energy in both the capacitors, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{U}_{f}&=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2})\mathrm{V}_{f}^{2}\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times10\left(\frac{2\mathrm{V}}{10}\right)^{2}=0.2\mathrm{~V}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ So, energy dissipated $$ \frac{V^{2}-0.2V^{2}}{V^{2}}\times100=80\% $$ - Let charge q on the capacitor (C) when connected to cell of emf (V) is given by, $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{q}{C} $$ Energy stored is, $ U = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2} = \frac{q^{2}}{2C} $ When dielectric slab of dielectric constant K is inserted, then capacitance $ C' $ will be $$ \mathbf{V}^{\prime}=\frac{\boldsymbol{q}}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{q}}{C\mathbf{K}} $$ And energy stored is, $ U' = \frac{q^{2}}{2CK} $ $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\mathbf{U}&=\mathbf{U}^{\prime}-\mathbf{U}\\&=\frac{q^{2}}{2C}\bigg(\frac{1}{\mathbf{K}}-1\bigg)\\&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}\bigg(\frac{1}{\mathbf{K}}-1\bigg)\end{aligned} $$ - The energy given to the cell, $$ E=C\mathbf{V}^{2} $$ where, $$ C=c a p a c i t a n c e o f c o n d u c t o r=\frac{A\varepsilon_{0}}{d} $$ V = potential difference across the plate $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{array}{c}{\mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}\varepsilon_{0}}{d}\right)(\mathrm{E}d)^{2}}\\ {\mathrm{=A}\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{E}^{2}d}\end{array} $$ - Work done in charging fully both the condensers, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}=\mathbf{U}&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathbf{C}}{2}\right)\mathbf{V}^{2}\\&=\frac{3}{4}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}\end{aligned} $$ - The initial energy held in a capacitor when it is charged by connecting a battery across its plates. $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{q^{2}}{2\mathrm{C}} $$ The charge remains constant when the battery is disconnected, i.e. $q = \text{constant}$. Now, another identical capacitor is connected across it, forming a parallel connection. Thus, the equivalent capacitance $$ \begin{aligned}C_{eq}&=C_{1}+C_{2}\\&=C+C=2\ C\end{aligned} $$ Final energy stored by the system of capacitors, $$ \begin{aligned}&U^{\prime}=\frac{q^{2}}{2C_{eq}}\\&U^{\prime}=\frac{q^{2}}{2(2C)}\\&U^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}U\\ \end{aligned} $$ - The energy stored in the capacitor is determined by $ U = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} $ If the initial and the final potential are $ V_{1} $ and $ V_{2} $ respectively, Then increase in energy ( $ \Delta U $), $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta U&=\frac{1}{2}C(V_{2}^{2}-V_{1}^{2})\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times(6\times10^{-6})\times[(20)^{2}-(10)^{2}]\\&=\frac{1}{2}(6\times10^{-6})\times300=9\times10^{-4}J\end{aligned} $$

title: Equipotential Surfaces
summary: It is given, $$ V=6x-8xy-8y+6yz $$ We know that, $$ \mathrm{E}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d r} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d x}=-6+8y $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Y}}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d y}=+8x+8-6z $$ $$…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - The angle between the electric lines of force and the equipotential surface is $ 90^{\circ} $. - Work done, $ W = q \Delta V $ ∆V is same in all the cases, so work done is same in all the cases.
##### Related Theory An equipotential surface is that at every point of which electric potential is the same and electric field lines of force are always perpendicular to an equipotential surface. - From question, Potential in a region, $$ \mathrm{V}_{(x,y,z)}=6x y-y+2y z $$ ∴ Electric field. $$ \begin{aligned}\vec{E}&=-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial x}\hat{i}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial y}\hat{j}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial z}\hat{k}\\&=[-6y\hat{i}-(6x-1+2z)\hat{j}-2y\hat{k}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\therefore\quad\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{(1,1,0)}&=-1(6\times1)\hat{i}-(6\times1-1+2\times0)\hat{j}-(2\times1)\hat{k}\\&=-(6\hat{i}+5\hat{j}+2\hat{k})\mathbf{N}/\mathrm{C}\end{aligned} $$ - It is given, $$ V=6x-8xy-8y+6yz $$ We know that, $$ \mathrm{E}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d r} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d x}=-6+8y $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Y}}=-\frac{d\mathbf{V}}{d y}=+8x+8-6z $$ $$ E_{z}=-\frac{d V}{d z}=-6y $$ The values of $ E_X $, $ E_Y $ and $ E_Z $ at $ (1, 1, 1) $ are, $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{X}}=-6+8\times1=+2 $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Y}}=8\times1+8-6\times1=+10 $$ $$ \mathrm{E_{Z}=-6~\times~1=-6} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{net}}&=\sqrt{(2)^{2}+(10)^{2}+(6)^{2}}\\&=\sqrt{4+100+36}=\sqrt{140}\\&=\sqrt{35\times4}=2\sqrt{35}\mathrm{~N/C}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{F}&=q,\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{net}}\\&=2\times(2\sqrt{35})\\&=4\sqrt{35}\mathrm{N}\end{aligned} $$ - It is given that, $ V = 4x^{2} $ then we have. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}&=-\left[\hat{i}\frac{\partial\mathrm{V}}{\partial x}+\hat{j}\frac{\partial\mathrm{V}}{\partial y}+\hat{k}\frac{\partial\mathrm{V}}{\partial z}\right]\\&=-\hat{i}\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(4x^{2})\end{aligned} $$ $$ =-\mathbf{8}\mathbf{x}\hat{i}~\mathbf{V}\mathbf{m}^{-2} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{E}_{(1,0,2)}=-8\times1\text{i}\\ =-\hat{8i}.\text{Vm}^{-1}\end{array} $$ - We know that. $$ \begin{aligned}\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{E}}&=-\frac{d\mathbf{N}}{d r}=\left[-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial x}\hat{i}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial y}\hat{j}-\frac{\partial\mathbf{V}}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right]\\&=\left[(2xy+z^{3})\hat{i}+x^{2}\hat{j}+3xz^{2}\hat{k}\right]\end{aligned} $$

title: Objective I
summary: Capacitance is $C = Q/V$, but $C$ is **not** a function of $Q$ or $V$ individually — it is determined entirely by: 1.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Capacitance is $C = Q/V$, but $C$ is **not** a function of $Q$ or $V$ individually — it is determined entirely by: 1. The geometry of the conductors (shape, size, separation) 2. The medium (dielectric) between them For a parallel-plate capacitor: $C = \varepsilon_0 A/d$ (vacuum) or $C = K\varepsilon_0 A/d$ (with dielectric $K$). Doubling $Q$ doubles $V$ proportionally, leaving $C = Q/V$ unchanged. Answer: (D).
- For a parallel-plate capacitor, the uniform electric field between the plates is $E = \sigma/\varepsilon_0 = Q/(\varepsilon_0 A)$. Voltage: $V = Ed = Qd/(\varepsilon_0 A)$. $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$ Capacitance increases with larger plate area and decreases with larger separation. Answer: (B).
- In series, the same charge $Q$ resides on each capacitor, and voltages add: $$V = V_1 + V_2 = \frac{Q}{C_1} + \frac{Q}{C_2} = Q\!\left(\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}\right)$$ $$C_{\rm eq} = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$ This is always **less than** the smaller of $C_1$, $C_2$. Answer: (B).
- Energy is stored in the electric field between the plates. Charging from 0 to $Q$: $$U = \int_0^Q \frac{q}{C}\,dq = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2}QV$$ All three forms are equivalent. Answer: (C).
- Inserting a dielectric of constant $K$ fills the field with a polarisable medium, reducing the effective field and thus increasing the capacitance: $$C' = KC = \frac{K\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$ With the battery **still connected**, $V$ is fixed, so $Q = C'V = KCV$ increases and energy $U = \frac{1}{2}C'V^2 = \frac{1}{2}KCV^2$ also increases (the battery supplies extra charge and energy). Answer: (B).
- Charge $Q$ is constant (battery disconnected). When $d$ doubles, $C' = C/2$. $$V' = \frac{Q}{C'} = \frac{Q}{C/2} = \frac{2Q}{C} = 2V$$ The voltage doubles. Intuitively: the same charge on plates farther apart → stronger field over a longer gap → twice the voltage. Answer: (C).
- Battery disconnected → $Q$ is constant. Inserting dielectric: $C \to KC$. $$U' = \frac{Q^2}{2C'} = \frac{Q^2}{2KC} = \frac{U}{K}$$ Since $K > 1$, $U' < U$: the energy **decreases**. Where does the lost energy go? It appears as work done by the electric field pulling the dielectric into the capacitor (the dielectric is attracted into the gap). Answer: (B).
- In parallel, capacitances add directly: $$C_{\rm eq} = C_1 + C_2 = 2 + 3 = 5\,\mu\rm F$$ Parallel connection: same voltage, charges add. Answer: (C).

title: Objective II
summary: After connecting: - **A** ✓: Charge is conserved: $Q_1 + 0 = C_1 V_f + C_2 V_f$, so $V_f = Q_1/(C_1+C_2)$.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Battery connected → voltage $V$ fixed throughout. - **A** ✓: $C \to KC > C$. - **B** ✓: $Q = CV \to KCV > CV$. - **C** ✗: Voltage is fixed by the battery. $V$ does not change. - **D** ✓: $U = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 \to \frac{1}{2}KCV^2 > U$. The extra charge and energy come from the battery. Answer: (A), (B), (D).
- After connecting: - **A** ✓: Charge is conserved: $Q_1 + 0 = C_1 V_f + C_2 V_f$, so $V_f = Q_1/(C_1+C_2)$. - **B** ✗: Energy before: $Q_1^2/(2C_1)$. Energy after: $Q_1^2/[2(C_1+C_2)]$. Energy after $<$ energy before — the difference is dissipated as heat (even in zero-resistance wires, via radiation/sparks during the transient). - **C** ✓: At equilibrium, both capacitors are at the same potential $V_f$. - **D** ✓: Energy is lost to heat (or radiation) in the connecting process. Answer: (A), (C), (D).
- - **A** ✓: For an isolated sphere: $C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 r$ (treating infinity as the other "plate"). - **B** ✓: Adding a grounded outer sphere reduces the effective radius of the "other plate" to a finite value, increasing $C$. - **C** ✓: $C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 r$ — larger $r$ → larger $C$. - **D** ✗: If the sphere is surrounded by a dielectric, $\varepsilon_0 \to K\varepsilon_0$, so $C$ depends on the medium. D is incorrect. Answer: (A), (B), (C).

title: Overview
summary: Work done, $ W = q \Delta V $ ∆V is same in all the cases, so work done is same in all the cases.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Since, electric potential remains constant inside the metallic spherical shell which is same as at the surface of spherical shell. Outside the spherical shell, $$ \mathrm{V}\propto\frac{1}{r}. $$ So, the graph given in option (B) is the variation of potential (V) with distance r. - Work done, $ W = q \Delta V $ ∆V is same in all the cases, so work done is same in all the cases.
##### Related Theory An equipotential surface is that at every point of which electric potential is the same and electric field lines of force are always perpendicular to an equipotential surface.

subtopic: Potential Energy in an External Field
title: Potential Energy in an External Field
summary: Force on each charge of dipole Charge of dipole $ F = qE $ \therefore Total force = zero, they are in opposite direction \therefore \overrightarrow{p} and \overrightarrow{E} are parallel to each other…
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Since, the electric field is growing against the dipole direction. Hence, the dipole will move towards the left due to decrease in its potential energy.
- Potential energy of dipole $$ \mathrm{U}=-p\mathrm{E}\left(\cos\theta_{2}-\cos\theta_{1}\right) $$ Here $$ \begin{aligned}\theta_{2}&=0^{\circ}\\&=-p\mathrm{E}\left(\cos90^{\circ}-\cos0^{\circ}\right)\\\mathrm{U}&=p\mathrm{E}\end{aligned} $$
- Work done, $$ \mathbf{W}=\underset{0}{\overset{\theta}{\int}}p\mathbf{E}\sin\theta\,d\theta $$ Since, $$ =p\mathrm{E}\left(1-\cos\theta\right) $$ $$ \theta=90^{\circ} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=p\mathrm{E}(1-\cos90^{\circ}) $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=p\mathbf{E} $$
- Force on each charge of dipole Charge of dipole $ F = qE $ \therefore Total force = zero, they are in opposite direction \therefore \overrightarrow{p} and \overrightarrow{E} are parallel to each other \overrightarrow{11} = -\overrightarrow{p} \cdot \overrightarrow{E} is minimum.

subtopic: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
title: Potential Energy of a System of Charges
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - $$ \mathbf{W}=\Delta\mathbf{U}=\mathbf{Q}\left(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{D}}-\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) $$ where, $$ \mathbf{V_{C}}=0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\mathbf{Q}\left[\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}(3\mathrm{L})}-\frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{L})}\right]\\&=-\frac{\mathbf{Q}q}{6\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{L}}\end{aligned} $$ Potential at C is zero because the charges are equal and opposite and the distances are the same. Potential at D due to -q is greater than due to +q, because D is closer to B. ∴ It is negative. - $$ \mathrm{U}_{i}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{1}q_{3}}{(0.4)}+\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{(0.3)}+\frac{q_{2}q_{3}}{(0.5)}\right] $$ $$ \mathbf{U}_{f}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q_{1}q_{3}}{(0.4)}+\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{(0.3)}+\frac{q_{2}q_{3}}{(0.1)}\right] $$
$$ \begin{array}{l}\Delta\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{f}-\mathrm{U}_{i}\\ =~\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}~q_{2}q_{3}~\left(\frac{1}{0.1}-\frac{1}{0.5}\right)\\ =~\frac{q_{2}q_{3}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}(8)\\ =~\frac{q_{3}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}(8q_{2}) \end{array} $$ On comparing with the equation given in question, we get $$ k=8q_{2} $$ -
Distance between the charge -q and +q is, $$ r=\sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{2}b)^{2}+(b)^{2}}{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}b $$ Electric potential energy of charge + q is, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=8\times\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q(-q)}{r}\\&=8\times\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q(-q)}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}b}\end{aligned} $$ $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{-4q^{2}}{\sqrt{3}\pi\varepsilon_{0}b} $$ - The electron is charged negatively. When an electron passes nearby another electron, the same negative charges produce a repulsive force between them. As a result, work is done against this repulsive force in order to bring them closer together. Electrostatic potential energy is used to store the work. As a result, the system's electrostatic potential energy increases. Since, Electrostatic potential energy of system of two electrons, $$ \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{(-e)(-e)}{r}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{e^{2}}{r} $$ Clearly, r decreases, potential energy U increases.

subtopic: Potential due to a Point Charge
title: Potential due to a Point Charge
summary: Work done is equal to zero because the potential of A and B are the same, i.e., $ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q}{a} $ <div style="text-align: center;"><img…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - As we know $ V = \frac{kq}{r} $ Given $ q = 4 \times 10^{-7} C $ $ r = 9 \, cm = 0.09 \, m $ $ V = \frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 4 \times 10^{-7}}{0.09} = 4 \times 10^{4} V $ - Since $$ \begin{array}{r l}{\mathrm{)~I s i n c e~}}&{{}}\\ {{\mathrm{~G i v e n~}}}&{{}}\\ {{\Rightarrow}}&{{}}\\ {{\mathrm{~N o w~}}}&{{}}\\ {{\Rightarrow}}&{{}}\\ \end{array}\quad\begin{array}{l}{\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r}}\\ {\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{Q}\times10^{11}\mathrm{~V}}\\ {r=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\times10^{11}}}\\ {\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{r}}\\ {\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{Q}\times10^{11}\times4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\times10^{11}}\\ {=4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{~Q}\times10^{22}\mathrm{~V}/\mathrm{m}}\end{array} $$ - Work done is equal to zero because the potential of A and B are the same, i.e., $ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q}{a} $
No work is done, if a particle does not change its potential energy. i.e., initial potential energy = final potential energy.

subtopic: Potential due to a System of Charges
title: Potential due to a System of Charges
summary: We know that surface charge density, $$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{\sigma}&=\frac{q}{A}=\frac{q}{4\pi R^{2}}\\q&=\sigma.4\pi R^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $\Rightarrow$ Before contact of both the spheres, <div…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - For a spherical shell $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{c e n t r e}}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{s u r f a c e}} $$ Difference in potential is zero. because the electric field intensity inside the shell is zero. This means that the work done in moving a point charge inside the shell is also zero. - For a hollow sphere, electric potential is, $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{k q}{\mathbf{R}} $$ For sphere $$ \mathbf{V}_{1}=\frac{k q}{\mathbf{R}_{1}} $$ For sphere 2, $ V_{2}=\frac{kq}{R_{2}} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{As}\;\mathrm{R}_{1}>>\mathrm{R}_{2},\\\therefore\quad\mathrm{V}_{1}\ll\mathrm{V}_{2}.\end{array} $$ So, potential is more on smaller sphere. - Given, Radius = R_{1} and R_{2} Surface charge densities are $ \sigma_{1} $ and $ \sigma_{2} $ Since, the spheres are connected by wire. Hence, the potential on them will be equal. $$ \begin{aligned}\mathrm{V}_{1}&=\mathrm{V}_{2}\\\frac{k q_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}&=\frac{k q_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\\\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}&=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\\q_{1}&=\sigma_{1}\mathrm{A}_{1},q_{2}=\sigma_{2}\mathrm{A}_{2}\\q_{1}&=\sigma_{1}4\pi\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}\\q_{2}&=\sigma_{2}4\pi\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}\\\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}&=\frac{\frac{q_{1}}{4\pi\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}}{\frac{q_{2}}{4\pi\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}}\\\frac{\sigma_{1}}{\sigma_{2}}&=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}\times\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}\end{aligned} $$ - Given, [Using (i)] Number of drops = 27 Radius of smaller sphere = r Voltage (V) = 220 V Total radius of bigger sphere = R Bigger sphere volume, $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{B}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi\mathbf{R}^{3} $$ Smaller sphere volume. $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} $$ 27 $$ \mathrm{\frac{4}{3}}\pi r^{3}=\mathrm{\frac{4}{3}}\pi R^{3} $$ $$ 27\;r^{3}=\mathrm{R}^{3} $$ ∴ $$ \boldsymbol{R}=3\boldsymbol{r} $$ Now, $$ \mathrm{V}_{0}=\frac{q_{1}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r} $$ Charge $q_{1}$ on smaller sphere, $$ q_{1}={\bf V}_{0}\:4\pi\epsilon_{0}r $$ Charge $q_{2}$ on bigger sphere, $$ \begin{aligned}q_{2}&=27q_{1}\\ V&=\frac{q_{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}R}\\&=\frac{27q_{1}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}3r}\\&=9V_{0}\\ V&=9\times220\\&=1980V\end{aligned} $$ - We know that surface charge density, $$ \begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{\sigma}&=\frac{q}{A}=\frac{q}{4\pi R^{2}}\\q&=\sigma.4\pi R^{2}\end{aligned} $$ $\Rightarrow$ Before contact of both the spheres,
$$ \begin{aligned}&q_{1}=\sigma.4\pi R^{2}\\&q_{2}=\sigma.4\pi(2R)^{2}\\&q_{2}=4q_{1}\\ \end{aligned} $$ After contact,
Here, $$ \sigma_{1}=\frac{q_{1}^{\prime}}{4\pi R^{2}} \text{ and} $$ $$ \sigma_{2}=\frac{q_{2}^{\prime}}{4\pi(2R)^{2}} $$ Again we know that, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{k q}{\mathbf{R}}\\\mathbf{V}&=\frac{k q_{1}^{\prime}}{\mathbf{R}}=\frac{k q_{2}^{\prime}}{2\mathbf{R}}\\q_{2}^{\prime}&=2q_{1}^{\prime}\end{aligned} $$ So So, after contact we can say, $$ q_{1}^{\prime}+q_{2}^{\prime}=q_{1}+q_{2}=q_{1}+4q_{1}=5q_{1} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c} \therefore\quad q^{\prime}_{1}+2q^{\prime}_{1}=5\times\sigma\ 4\pi R^{2}\\ \Rightarrow\quad3q^{\prime}_{1}=5\times\sigma\times4\pi R^{2}\\ \end{array} $$ and $$ q_{2}^{\prime}=2q_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{10}{3}\times\sigma4\pi R^{2} $$ Putting the value of $q_{1}^{\prime}$ and $q_{2}^{\prime}$ in eqns (ii) & (iii), we get $$ \sigma_{1}=\frac{5}{3}\sigma\text{and}\sigma_{2}=\frac{5}{6}\sigma $$ - According to the question, When potential at centre is zero, then - The situation is shown in the diagram,
From the figure. $$ AC=BC(given) $$ D and E are midpoints of BC and AC. ∴ Using geometry it can be proved that BE = DA. $$ \mathrm{V_{D}=V_{E}=V} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{W}&=\mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{D}})\\&=\mathbf{Q}(\mathbf{V}-\mathbf{V})\\\mathbf{W}&=0\end{aligned} $$
$$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{k q}{\mathrm{L}}+\frac{k q}{\mathrm{L}}-\frac{k q}{\sqrt{5}\mathrm{L}}-\frac{k q}{\sqrt{5}\mathrm{L}}\\ =\frac{2k q}{\mathrm{L}}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\\ =\frac{2q}{\mathrm{L}}\times\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\end{array} $$ -
$$ \begin{aligned}&\mathrm{V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}+\mathrm{V}_{3}+\mathrm{V}_{4}=0\\-\frac{\mathrm{KQ}}{\frac{r}{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{Kq}}{\frac{r}{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}(2\mathrm{Q})}{\frac{r}{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{K}2q}{\frac{r}{2}}=0\\\Rightarrow\quad&\quad-\mathrm{Q}-q+2q+2\mathrm{Q}=0\\\Rightarrow\quad&\quad\mathrm{Q}=-q\end{aligned} $$ - $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{q_{\mathrm{A}}}{a}+\frac{q_{\mathrm{B}}}{b}+\frac{q_{\mathrm{C}}}{c}\right\} $$
$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{a^{2}\sigma}{a}-\frac{b^{2}\sigma}{b}+\frac{c^{2}\sigma}{c}\right\} $$ $$ \mathbf{V_{B}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{a^{2}\sigma}{b}-\frac{b^{2}\sigma}{b}+\frac{c^{2}\sigma}{c}\right\} $$ and $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left\{\frac{a^{2}\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{c}-\frac{b^{2}\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{c}+\frac{c^{2}\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{c}\right\} $$ Using, c = a + b, $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{2a\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{b}+a\right)=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{ac}{b} $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V_{C}}&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\Bigg[\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}+c^{2}}{c}\Bigg]\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\Bigg[\frac{a^{2}-b^{2}+(a+b)^{2}}{(a+b)}\Bigg]\quad\ldots(\mathrm{iii})\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\Bigg[\frac{(a-b)(a+b)+(a+b)(a+b)}{(a+b)}\Bigg]\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\times2a\quad\{\text{since}c=a+b\}\end{aligned} $$ So, from eqns (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{A}}=\mathbf{V}_{C}\neq\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{B}} $$ - Let the charges on the spheres be Q and q respectively. The potential at common center, $$ \begin{array}{c} \mathbf{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathbf{Q}}{\mathbf{R}}\right)\;+\;\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathbf{q}}{r}\right)\\ =\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{\mathbf{Q}}{4\pi\mathbf{R}^{2}}\times\mathbf{R}+\frac{\mathbf{q}}{4\pi r^{2}}\times\mathbf{r}\right] \end{array} $$ But, $$ \frac{Q}{4\pi R^{2}}=\frac{q}{4\pi r^{2}}=\sigma $$ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\boldsymbol{\sigma}\mathbf{R}+\boldsymbol{\sigma}\mathbf{r}\right]\\&=\frac{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left(\mathbf{R}+\mathbf{r}\right)\end{aligned} $$

formulas.latex: \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{c e n t r e}}=\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{s u r f a c e}}

subtopic: Potential due to an Electric Dipole
title: Potential due to an Electric Dipole
summary: Given $$ p=16\times10^{-9}C m $$ Distance, r = 0.6 m, Angle, $ \theta = 60^{\circ} $ $$ 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} $$ The electric potential at a point at a distance r at some angle $\theta$ from electric dipole is V.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Formula of potential is given by $$ \mathbf{V}=\frac{k\varphi\cos\theta}{r^{2}} $$ For axial point, $ \theta = 0^{\circ} $ or $ 180^{\circ} $ $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{V}=\pm\frac{k p}{r^{2}}=\pm\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{p}{r^{2}}\\ =\pm\frac{9\times10^{9}\times4\times10^{-6}}{4}\\ =9\times10^{3}\text{V}\end{array} $$
- Given $$ p=16\times10^{-9}C m $$ Distance, r = 0.6 m, Angle, $ \theta = 60^{\circ} $ $$ 60^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} $$ The electric potential at a point at a distance r at some angle $\theta$ from electric dipole is V. and $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}&=\frac{p\cos\theta}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}}\\&=9\times10^{9}\times\frac{16\times10^{-9}\times\frac{1}{2}}{(0.6)^{2}}\\&=2\times10^{2}=200\end{aligned} $$

title: The Parallel Plate Capacitor
summary: On keeping the battery connected voltage remains same $$ \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon\mathrm{A}}{d},\mathrm{If}d\mathrm{~is~reduced}, $$ ∴ capacitance increases.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- On keeping the battery connected voltage remains same $$ \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon\mathrm{A}}{d},\mathrm{If}d\mathrm{~is~reduced}, $$ ∴ capacitance increases. From $$ Q=CV $$ If C is increased, the product of capacitance and voltage also increases, so charge also increases. E = $ \frac{1}{2} $ CV $ ^{2} $, As C increases, energy also increases. So, statement (I), (III) and (V) are correct.
- We know that, For isolated capacitor, Q = constant $$ \mathrm{F_{plate}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{2A\varepsilon_{0}} $$ So. F is independent of the distance between the plates.
- According to question, $$ \mathbf{F}=\frac{\mathbf{Q}^{2}}{2\varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}} $$ $$ \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV}~\mathrm{and}~\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d} $$ $$ \varepsilon_{0}\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{C}d $$ From eqn. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\ast $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathbf{C}^{2}\mathbf{V}^{2}}{2\mathrm{Cd}}=\frac{\mathbf{C}\mathbf{V}^{2}}{2d} $$
- According to the question q = Constant and decrease in C means increase in V. $$ \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{A}}{d} $$ $$ Capacitance=\frac{Charge}{Potential difference} $$ $$ \Rightarrow\mathrm{Potential}\ \mathrm{difference}=\frac{\mathrm{Charge}}{\mathrm{Capacitance}}. $$

title: reNEET2026
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Evaluating the charge and energy distribution for the shown network, the energy in P is one-sixth of the total stored energy, $U_P/U_T=\tfrac16.$
- For a unit charge $|W|=|V_B-V_A|$. Using the potential of the uniformly charged sphere between the two points gives $|W|=\dfrac{\rho R^2}{18\,\varepsilon_0}$, so $n=18.$
- Energy conservation $3R\to R$: $\tfrac12 mv^2=\dfrac{Qq}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\dfrac1R-\dfrac1{3R}\right)=\dfrac{Qq}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{2}{3R}$, giving $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{Qq}{3\pi\varepsilon_0 mR}}.$
